questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of tingling in her fingers and toes, followed by weakness in her legs. She had a diarrhoeal illness 2 weeks ago. On examination, she has symmetrical weakness in her lower limbs (MRC grade 4/5 proximally, 3/5 distally), absent ankle jerks, and mild loss of vibration sense in the toes. Cranial nerves are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The acute onset after a gastrointestinal infection, ascending symmetrical weakness, areflexia, and mild sensory loss are classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome. CIDP has a slower, relapsing course; diabetic and alcoholic neuropathies are chronic; B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration with prominent proprioceptive loss.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with burning pain in both feet, worse at night. He has reduced sensation to monofilament in the soles, absent ankle jerks, and mild hammer toe deformities. Peripheral pulses are palpable. Blood tests show HbA1c 8.0%, normal B12 and TSH. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The symmetrical distal sensory loss, absent reflexes, and burning pain in a diabetic patient with good pulses point to diabetic polyneuropathy. PAD would have absent pulses; tarsal tunnel is unilateral; B12 deficiency has proprioceptive loss; lumbar radiculopathy is dermatomal.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to A&E with acute abdominal pain, vomiting, and tachycardia. Over 24 hours, she develops ascending weakness in her limbs, and her urine is noted to be dark in colour. On examination, she has symmetrical proximal weakness, areflexia, and mild sensory loss. She is confused and agitated. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of acute abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms, autonomic instability (tachycardia), and ascending neuropathy is classic for acute porphyria. Guillain-Barré lacks abdominal pain; myasthenia gravis has fatiguability without sensory loss; botulism descends; conversion disorder has inconsistent signs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known asthma and nasal polyps presents with rapid-onset foot drop in the left foot, followed by wrist drop in the right hand over 2 weeks. He has lost weight and has joint pains. On examination, he has multiple mononeuropathies and a purpuric rash on his legs. His eosinophil count is 2.0 ×10^9/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex (due to eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
questions.explanation: The asymmetric, stepwise involvement of multiple peripheral nerves (mononeuritis multiplex) with eosinophilia, asthma, and purpura strongly suggests eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome). GBS is symmetric; MS is central; MND is pure motor; diabetic amyotrophy is proximal and unilateral.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 2-year history of progressive numbness and tingling in his hands and feet, unsteady gait, and mild distal weakness. He has no history of diabetes or alcohol excess. On examination, he has impaired vibration and proprioception in the toes, Romberg positive, and areflexia. Blood tests reveal a monoclonal IgG kappa band on serum electrophoresis, normal B12 and folate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The chronic, slowly progressive symmetrical sensory-predominant polyneuropathy with monoclonal gammopathy and normal B12 is typical of MGUS-related neuropathy. B12 deficiency causes more pronounced proprioceptive loss (subacute combined degeneration); diabetic neuropathy usually has burning pain; CIDP is relapsing-remitting; amyloid often has autonomic involvement and macroglossia.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with burning pain in both feet for 1 year. She has no weakness, and reflexes are intact. Nerve conduction studies are normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy for intraepidermal nerve fiber density is the gold standard for diagnosing small fibre neuropathy when nerve conduction studies are normal. The most tempting distractor is quantitative sudomotor axon reflex test, which assesses autonomic function but is less specific for small fibre damage.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman has had slowly progressive foot drop and mild distal hand weakness over several years. She has high-arched feet and her father has similar symptoms. Nerve conduction studies show uniformly reduced conduction velocities. Which investigation is most likely to provide a definitive diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The uniform slowing on nerve conduction studies and family history strongly suggest Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. Genetic testing can identify the specific mutation, providing a definitive diagnosis. Nerve biopsy is rarely needed and would not be first-line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute ascending weakness over 3 days, areflexia, and mild sensory loss. She is unable to walk. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The clinical picture is classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome. Lumbar puncture showing albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count) is characteristic and helps confirm the diagnosis. Nerve conduction studies are supportive but may be normal early.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes develops rapidly progressive mononeuritis multiplex, weight loss, and a raised ESR. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the underlying cause?
questions.explanation: The presentation suggests vasculitic neuropathy secondary to systemic vasculitis. Sural nerve biopsy demonstrating necrotizing vasculitis provides definitive confirmation. Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies are supportive but not diagnostic alone.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man has a history of bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, mild peripheral neuropathy, and left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram. He has no family history of neuropathy. Which investigation is most likely to establish the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of carpal tunnel syndrome, neuropathy, and cardiomyopathy is classic for transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR). Genetic testing for TTR mutation is first-line, as hereditary ATTR is more common than wild-type at this age and can present without family history. Serum immunofixation would screen for AL amyloid but is less likely given no paraprotein.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with burning pain and paraesthesiae in both feet for 3 months. Examination shows reduced vibration sensation and absent ankle jerks. Nerve conduction studies confirm a symmetrical axonal neuropathy. He has moderate pain (6/10). What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Duloxetine is recommended by NICE as first-line for painful diabetic neuropathy due to its efficacy and favourable side-effect profile compared to amitriptyline, which is also first-line but often less tolerated. Pregabalin and gabapentin are second-line options.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with ascending symmetrical weakness and areflexia over 5 days, following a diarrhoeal illness. CSF shows elevated protein with normal cell count. A diagnosis of Guillain-Barré syndrome is made. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: IVIG is the first-line therapy in Guillain-Barré syndrome as per NICE guidelines; it is as effective as plasma exchange but easier to administer. Corticosteroids are not beneficial and may worsen outcomes.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute severe abdominal pain, tachycardia, and hypertension. Urine dipstick is positive for porphobilinogen. She develops ascending weakness and areflexia over 24 hours. A diagnosis of acute porphyric neuropathy is made. What is the most appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: Haem arginate is the specific treatment for acute porphyria attacks as it suppresses hepatic ALA synthase activity. IV dextrose is supportive but not first-line for neuropathy. Opioids are for analgesia but not definitive therapy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) has responded well to initial induction therapy. He now requires maintenance treatment to prevent relapses. He has no contraindications to systemic steroids. What is the most appropriate first-line maintenance therapy?
questions.explanation: In CIDP, corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) are a first-line maintenance therapy due to their oral route and efficacy. IVIG is also first-line but often reserved if steroids fail or are contraindicated. Azathioprine and rituximab are second-line or third-line.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with slowly progressive distal paraesthesiae and gait ataxia. She has IgM kappa monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) and a serum antibody titre against myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG). Nerve conduction studies show demyelinating neuropathy. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: Rituximab is first-line for anti-MAG neuropathy, as it targets the B-cell clone producing the pathogenic IgM. Steroids and IVIG have limited efficacy. Cyclophosphamide is reserved for refractory cases due to toxicity.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of ascending weakness in his legs, now unable to walk unaided. He had a diarrhoeal illness 2 weeks ago. Examination reveals areflexia in both legs and mild bilateral facial weakness. Vital signs: BP 130/80, HR 90, RR 18, SpO2 98% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected Guillain-Barré syndrome, the immediate priority is to assess respiratory function because respiratory failure is the leading cause of death. Measuring forced vital capacity (FVC) identifies patients at risk of needing ventilatory support before other investigations are performed.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of slowly progressive numbness and tingling in her feet. She has no pain, no weakness, and no falls. Examination shows absent ankle jerks, reduced vibration sense to the knees, and mild distal sensory loss. Blood tests including FBC, U&E, LFT, B12, folate, TSH, HbA1c, and serum protein electrophoresis are normal. Nerve conduction studies show an axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order MRI of the whole spine
Repeat nerve conduction studies in 6 months
questions.explanation: In chronic idiopathic axonal polyneuropathy (CIAP), after excluding common causes, a neurologist should be involved to consider rarer etiologies (e.g., vasculitis, hereditary) and to confirm the diagnosis. Starting neuropathic pain agents is not indicated without pain. MRI spine is low yield without signs of myelopathy.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 8 years ago) complains of burning pain in both feet for 6 months, which keeps her awake at night. Examination reveals reduced pinprick sensation to the mid-shin and stocking-distribution loss of vibration sense. Ankle jerks are present. She has no ulceration. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Advise on well-fitting shoes and foot care
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend amitriptyline, duloxetine, or pregabalin as first-line for painful diabetic neuropathy. Starting amitriptyline 10mg nocte is the immediate step to manage the pain. Checking HbA1c is important but not the most immediate for pain relief; referral to podiatry and advice are routine but not urgent.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of progressive right wrist drop and left foot drop. He also reports weight loss, night sweats, and a purpuric rash on his legs. Examination confirms these deficits. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex, prompt immunosuppression with high-dose steroids is critical to halt disease progression. While investigations (ANCA, NCS) are important, starting prednisolone immediately is the most urgent action to prevent further nerve damage. Biopsy may be done later.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with known Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A (confirmed) presents to the clinic complaining of increasing difficulty walking and tripping over his toes. Examination shows bilateral foot drop, pes cavus, and distal wasting. He has no pain. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to neurologist for disease-modifying therapy
Refer for surgical correction of foot deformity
Order nerve conduction studies to assess progression
questions.explanation: In CMT, management is supportive. An ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) is the most immediate intervention to improve gait and prevent falls in foot drop. No disease-modifying therapy exists. Nerve conduction studies are unnecessary as diagnosis is known. Surgery is not first-line and not immediate.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of symmetrical ascending weakness and sensory loss in his legs, areflexia, and mild back pain. He is otherwise well. Which single clinical finding would be the most important red flag for impending life-threatening deterioration?
questions.explanation: Reduced forced vital capacity indicates respiratory muscle weakness, the most immediate life-threatening complication of Guillain-Barré syndrome. Bulbar weakness (facial, ptosis) is concerning but less immediately life-threatening than respiratory failure. Urinary retention suggests spinal cord involvement requiring MRI to exclude myelitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known rheumatoid arthritis presents with acute onset right foot drop and left wrist drop over 2 weeks. She has no other new symptoms. Which single clinical finding would be the most important red flag for a life-threatening systemic vasculitis requiring urgent immunosuppression?
questions.explanation: Palpable purpura indicates small-vessel leucocytoclastic vasculitis, which can rapidly involve kidneys, lungs, and other organs. Mononeuritis multiplex is already present and is the presentation, not a new red flag. Cranial nerve involvement is also common in vasculitic neuropathies but does not carry the same systemic urgency as cutaneous vasculitis (which often signals renal or pulmonary involvement).

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute, severe, generalised abdominal pain and new-onset peripheral neuropathy with proximal weakness and areflexia. Which single clinical finding should raise the strongest suspicion for acute intermittent porphyria as the underlying cause?
questions.explanation: Reddish urine that darkens on exposure to light is highly specific for acute porphyrias. Hyponatraemia, psychosis, and family history are all associated but less specific. Preceding infection is more typical of Guillain-Barré.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) has been on long-term prednisolone 20 mg daily for 2 years. He recently ran out of medication 3 days ago. He now presents with vomiting and confusion. Which single clinical finding would be the most important red flag for life-threatening adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explanation: Postural hypotension is a classic sign of adrenal crisis due to mineralocorticoid deficiency, and in this setting with steroid withdrawal, it is the most dangerous finding indicating circulatory collapse. Hyperglycaemia and hyperkalaemia are not typical; steroids cause hyperglycaemia but withdrawal leads to hypoglycaemia and hyponatraemia. Peripheral oedema suggests fluid overload. Bilateral popliteal weakness may be part of his CIDP but not an acute emergency.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a recent diagnosis of monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) presents with a 6-month history of progressive symmetrical peripheral neuropathy with autonomic symptoms such as orthostatic dizziness and erectile dysfunction. Which single clinical finding would most strongly suggest underlying systemic amyloidosis?
questions.explanation: Macroglossia is a classic and specific finding for amyloidosis, often appearing before other organ involvement. Carpal tunnel syndrome is common but not as specific (can be idiopathic). Proteinuria and cardiomegaly are also present in amyloidosis but macroglossia is more immediately recognisable as a diagnostic clue.
